summary: Gruber lays out his expectations for Apple's Snow Leopard (Mac OS X 10.6) announcement at WWDC 2008, predicting it will drop PowerPC support, push 64-bit without dropping 32-bit, keep Carbon APIs despite 'pure Cocoa' rhetoric, and focus on refinement over new features. He argues the PowerPC cut, while unpopular, is justified by the hardware's age and the engineering simplicity it enables. He dismisses the idea that Carbon will be dropped entirely, noting the dependence of major apps like Photoshop and Excel on it. He also predicts Apple will never put touchscreens on Macs, distinguishing Mac OS X from a future touch-optimized OS, and suggests Snow Leopard could be sold as a refinement release that plays to Apple's strengths against Vista.
key_points: Snow Leopard will drop PowerPC support — aggressive but justified by the three-year hardware age at ship date and the engineering simplification it enables
64-bit is a talking point but 32-bit support will remain, since many Intel Macs don't even support 64-bit and thousands of apps depend on it
The 'pure Cocoa' messaging is an admonition to developers, not a signal that Carbon APIs will be removed — major apps like Photoshop and Excel depend on Carbon
Touchscreen Macs won't happen because the Mac UI isn't designed for it; adding touch to a mouse-and-keyboard UI is 'lipstick on a pig'
A no-new-features release can be sold by emphasizing the qualities that differentiate Mac OS X from Vista: elegance, simplicity, efficiency, and reliability
money_quotes: Dropping PowerPC support would significantly simplify the development and QA testing for Snow Leopard.
Dropping PowerPC support would be aggressive; dropping support for 18-month-old Intel machines would be insane.
If you thought it was bad when Photoshop and Excel only ran under Rosetta, imagine if they didn't run at all. Crazy talk.
Adding touchscreen support to a user interface designed for traditional mouse-and-keyboard access is a lipstick-on-a-pig design.
I don't expect to ever see touchscreen Macs. Touchscreen computers from Apple running OS X? Yes, I think, probably someday soon. But not Mac OS X.
The appeal of Mac OS X versus Windows is what? That it has more features? No. It's that it is more elegant, simpler, more efficient, and more reliable.
Apple could press their current advantage by emphasizing efficiency, elegance, simplicity, and reliability.
key_insight: Snow Leopard's 'no new features' approach is not a retreat but a strategic play — emphasizing refinement over feature bloat is exactly how Apple differentiates from Vista-era Microsoft.
